title: How Should Investors Respond to the Sea Change in US Monetary Policy?

The Fed deciding to cut rates in its
final decision of 2025. As we look ahead
to 2026, the FOMC is maintaining its
projection of only one cut in 2026, also
in 2027. Here to discuss this and more
is Darius Dale. He is the founder and
CEO of 42 Macro, a macro research firm.
Great to see you in person, Darius. So,
you think about what we got yesterday.
Uh the expectation was that we were
going to get a cut, perhaps a hawkish
cut, but listening to the press
conference, I'm not quite sure if that's
what was delivered.
>> Well, thanks for having me. Appreciate
you guys for being on. Uh in our
opinion, that might have been one of the
most historic FOMC events in our
lifetimes. This is a Federal Reserve
that in the midst of a nationwide
affordability crisis effectively said
>> we have to expand our balance sheet
>> and we have to give the repo market the
liquidity that it needs for hedge fund
leverage and retail margin debt to
continue expanding. And so in my opinion
that represents a significant sea change
in monetary policy in terms of the Fed's
reaction function. A reaction function
that we already thought was going to
change anyway next year as a function of
the next uh Fed year. Well, talk to us a
little bit more about that because it's
uh difficult messaging, right? They're
buying Treasury bills, but uh it's
really being cast as reserve management,
but you still have the folks on social
media and the like saying that no, this
is QE, including yourself.
>> Including myself. I should have brought
my bubble [laughter]
my bubble. Uh no, look, I mean, I'm
being tongue and cheek about it, but
what it does represent is it represents
an erosion of central bank independence.
Now, they're going to couch it as
reserve management operations or
whatever they want to couch it as, but
the reality is is the federal
government's balance sheet and the
growth of the federal government's
balance sheet has grown. It's gotten too
large. And so, ultimately, we need the
Fed to take pressure off the system in
order to, you know, help sustain the
bull markets and assets, the same growth
in the in the real economy. And
ultimately, this again, like I said,
this is a very significant change in US
monetary policy.
>> It gets to this idea though is whether
the market can ever stand on its own
again. It's funny before uh this show I
was having a conversation off camera
with someone about the global financial
crisis, the start of QE, QE2, QE
affinity and you know everything else in
between and it gets to this idea that
the Fed does try to pull back from time
to time but has never really found a way
to permanently pull back and I'm
wondering if we ever get back to that.
>> It seems unlikely from this starting
point. Uh I think that we're probably
multiple market and economic cycles away
from having that you know sort of come
to Jesus moment with monetary policy.
But between now and then, if you look
about from a from a risk-taking
perspective, from our perspective and
the perspective of many of the top
institutional investors on global Wall
Street, there's really only six things
that impact asset markets. Growth,
inflation, monetary policy, fiscal
policy, liquidity, and positioning.
Growth's already improving. Inflation is
going down. It's a tailwind currently.
We just saw the change in monetary
policy. That's good. Fiscal policy has
been tightening, but it's going to be
easing significantly next year. The
liquidity cycle is already still in an
uptrend, and the Fed is adding more
liquidity to the system. So the only one
of the five cycles that's an issue right
now is the positioning cycle and we see
historic degree of crowded bullish
positioning but we know the positioning
cycle is not a catalyst. So when you
have all that good news and all the
other key macro cycles that matters it
suggests that the risk is that the
markets could start to bubble.
>> It's interesting you say that too
because we have seen a little bit of a
rotation in US public equity markets
away from that high concentration in
tech and AI but that is still the
dominant theme. And I am curious because
this came up in the press conference
yesterday with Pal. Uh it was brought up
today in an interview on Bloomberg with
Robert Kaplan, a former Fed uh governor.
And it gets to this idea as to whether
that AI trade and the AI buildout is now
a macro economic story and one a story
that would have to be dealt with to a
certain degree by the Fed.
>> Yeah. Well, I the Fed's going to get
drag kicking and screaming to deal with
it anyway. But our take on AI is this.
We're going to at the end of this
rainbow, whatever it is, whe it's going
to be a secular bare market in either
the stock equity market or a secular
bare market in society. And so, right
now, I think the starting point you need
to recognize is that we are very much in
the early innings of what could
potentially be a bubble. You don't want
to be short that. You don't want to be
fighting that. I know that investors
look at valuations, but they're not
factoring in all these positive
liquidity and monetary dynamics, the
fiscal policy dynamics, as well as the
free cash flow the index currently
generates relative to what it did back
in the 90s.
>> Interesting. So, you're saying in your
view that bubble, which a lot of people
would have an instinct to maybe lean
away from or short. Uh, but in your
view, it sounds like it's just going to
expand from here.
>> When bubbles are inflating, you want to
belong them. [laughter]
>> When bubbles are inflating, go buy them.
>> It's pretty straightforward.
>> Yeah. You the problem the problem is is
you're trying to time it so you don't
get caught up in
>> Can I say something on that? I don't
believe in market timing whatsoever.
Return followers at 42 macro
quantitative investors.
>> Yeah.
>> I don't believe that you can sell the
exact top of the market. I don't believe
you can buy the exact bottom of the
market, but what you can do is sell near
the top and buy near the bottom with
trend following processes like ours.
>> All right. Well, well said Darius.
Always great to have you. Darius Dale is
the founder and CEO of 42 Macro. When we
come back, we are going to talk a little
bit more about that AI trade and the big
downdraft today in Oracle. The earnings
are out and the red flags are up. Stick
with [music] us. This is the close on
Bloomberg.
